g.data-narration: Welcome to File System Implementation - where we explore how operating systems organize and manage data on storage devices.
File systems bridge the gap between how users see files and how computers physically store data on disks and storage devices.
g.text: OS sees: Disk blocks, sectors, addresses
File system translates logical file operations into physical storage operations

g.data-narration: File systems are organized in layers - from user commands down to physical hardware, each layer handles specific responsibilities.
A disk is divided into blocks - each block is a fixed-size unit. The layout includes boot block, superblock, i-nodes, and data blocks.
g.text: Storage device divided into fixed-size blocks (512B - 4KB)

g.data-narration: Contiguous allocation stores each file in consecutive disk blocks. It's fast but suffers from external fragmentation - free space breaks into small pieces.
g.text: Each file occupies consecutive blocks on disk

g.data-narration: Linked allocation treats each file as a chain of blocks - each block points to the next. No fragmentation but poor random access performance.
g.text: Files as linked list of blocks - each block contains pointer to next

g.data-narration: Indexed allocation uses a separate index block containing pointers to all file blocks. Supports direct access efficiently but wastes space for small files.
g.text: Index block contains pointers to all file data blocks

g.data-narration: UNIX uses i-nodes with direct, single indirect, double indirect, and triple indirect pointers to support both small and very large files efficiently.
Bit vectors track free blocks with a simple 0 (free) and 1 (allocated) system. Efficient for finding contiguous space but needs memory.
g.text: Each block represented by 1 bit: 0 = free, 1 = allocated

g.data-narration: Linked list groups free blocks together - each free block points to the next. Grouping stores multiple free block addresses together for faster allocation.
g.text: Linked List: Free blocks point to next free block
OS keeps pointer to first free block
Grouping: Store addresses of free blocks in free blocks
